1:
The parable of the labourers in the vineyard divides the cloud of witnesses along a clear fault-line: the fathers read it as a sweeping allegory of salvation-history, while the modern and Reformed commentators rein it in to its one stated point — that ‘the last shall be first’ (v.16).

The Latin and Greek fathers mine every detail. Gregory the Great and Pseudo-Chrysostom read the hours as the ages of the world — morning (Adam to Noah), the third, sixth and ninth hours (Noah, Abraham, Moses), the eleventh the coming of Christ and the call of the Gentiles — or, alternatively, as the stages of human life from childhood to decrepit old age. Remigius, Jerome and the Gloss take the householder as Christ (or the Father working through the Son), the vineyard as the Church, the denarius as the reward of eternal life stamped, like a coin, with the King’s image. Origen (Alexandrian) calls the whole present life one day and the marketplace all that lies outside the Church. Augustine adds the refinement that, though all receive the one denarius of eternal life, ‘there are with the Father many mansions’ (compare John 14:2), so that one shines more than another.

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the framework and resist excess. Barnes insists the kingdom ‘has reference to rewards,’ and ties the vineyard to Isaiah 5:7 and Jeremiah 12:10. Ellicott laments the chapter division and roots the parable in the preceding story of the rich young ruler and the disciples’ ‘What shall we have?’ (Matthew 19:27), reading the householder as Christ and the labourers as the ‘hired servants’ of Luke 15:17. Robertson notes the classic idiom for break of day.

The Reformed Calvin sharply curbs the allegory: the parable is only a confirmation of the saying that the first have ‘no right to boast or to insult others,’ for God may call latecomers and make them equal; to ‘sift out with exactness every portion’ is ‘useless curiosity.’ The Puritan Henry and Wesleyan Wesley hold both senses together — the rejection of the Jews and call of the Gentiles, and the general truth that God is debtor to no man.
2:
‘He agreed with the labourers for a penny a day.’ The witnesses here are the grammatical-historical school, with Lightfoot and Calvin, and they devote themselves chiefly to the value of the coin and the nature of the contract.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the ‘penny’ as the Roman denarius. Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott, Robertson and Wesley agree it was worth about sevenpence-halfpenny in their currency, the common wage of a day-labourer and of a Roman soldier, and Ellicott stresses that its true equivalent lies in its purchasing power — ‘an adequate and just payment.’ Robertson prefers ‘shilling’ to ‘penny,’ and parses the construction as denoting the agreement struck with the workmen. Clarke piles up medieval English prices to show how a penny once sufficed for a day. The coin reappears in Matthew 18:28.

Lightfoot, with his rabbinic learning, distinguishes between those ‘hired by the day’ and those hired only ‘for some hours,’ noting that the canons of the Hebrews made just this distinction — the first labourers were engaged for the whole day and for a fixed penny, the later ones for a part-penny in proportion to the time worked. This observation, he thinks, illumines why a fixed sum is named only with the first.

The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note attached here in fact belongs to the discussion of the popular opinions about Jesus in Matthew 14:1, and so does not bear on the verse. The shared, dominant note across the genuine voices is that the wage was a real and just day’s pay, freely contracted; the bargain struck with the first labourers becomes, in the unfolding of the parable, the very thing that exposes their later murmuring as groundless, for they receive exactly what they agreed to. Gill cites the rabbinic category of those ‘hired for a day,’ and the witnesses agree that the fixed sum named here, set against the open ‘whatsoever is right’ offered to the later labourers, establishes the parable’s contrast between contract and trust; the wage itself, all concur, was neither mean nor excessive but the just hire of a single day’s toil. The bargain freely struck, all agree, becomes the very ground on which the later murmuring is shown to be groundless.
3:
‘He went out about the third hour, and saw others standing idle in the marketplace.’ The few witnesses, all grammatical-historical, agree on the hour and the scene, with a hint of the parable’s spiritual reference.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the time and place. Gill, Barnes and Clarke explain that the Jews divided the day into twelve hours from sunrise, so the third hour was about nine in the morning. Barnes notes the marketplace was where provisions were sold and where, therefore, employers and the unemployed would naturally meet — the idle were ‘not disposed to be idle, but were waiting in the proper place to find employers.’ Robertson vividly cites a traveller’s account of peasants gathering before sunrise at Hamadan in Persia, ‘with spades in their hands, waiting to be hired for the day.’

Ellicott, reckoning the Jewish day from six in the morning, brings the third hour to about eight, and presses the spiritual meaning: in the labourers called after the first he sees a reference to those summoned later, like Matthew (Matthew 9:9) and the disciples called in Matthew 8:19-22, after the sons of Jonas and Zebedee. Thus the staggered calls picture the successive summons of men into the kingdom.

With so brief and harmonious a body of comment, the synthesis is necessarily short, but the witnesses agree on the substance: the householder’s repeated journeys to the marketplace throughout the day display a grace that keeps seeking labourers, and the ‘idle’ are those who have not yet been called, waiting in the place where the call is given. The standing in the marketplace is, on the dominant reading, the natural posture of those whom the gospel has not yet reached, ready to be hired the moment the householder appears. The witnesses agree the men were idle not by sloth but for want of an employer, waiting in the very place where they might be hired; and that the householder’s coming out to them, rather than their coming to him, displays again the initiative of a grace that keeps seeking labourers throughout the day. The standing in the marketplace is thus the natural posture of those whom the gospel has not yet reached, ready to be hired the moment the householder appears before them.
4:
‘Go ye also into the vineyard, and whatsoever is right I will give you.’ The witnesses, grammatical-historical with the Reformed Gill, fasten on the absence of a fixed wage and the trust it implies.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the change of terms. Barnes notes that the contract with the first was definite, but with these it ‘depended on the judgment of the employer’ — ‘whatsoever it shall appear you can earn.’ Robertson glosses ‘whatsoever is right’ as ‘a just proportionate wage,’ not anything the householder pleased. Ellicott presses the spiritual point hardest: the absence of a definite contract ‘involved an implicit trust in the equity of the householder.’ These labourers did not stipulate for wages, nor ask, as the disciples had asked, ‘What shall we have therefore?’ (Matthew 19:27); and the implied lesson is that ‘a little work done, when God calls us, in the spirit of trust, is better than much done in the spirit of a hireling.’

The Reformed Gill reads the call typologically as ‘a call of divine grace out of the world into the church,’ arising from mere goodwill without any merit in man: the householder goes out to these men, not they to him; he questions and calls and bids them go, while ‘they do not ask him to hire them.’ The persons called, Gill notes, are ‘a parcel of idle, mean, vulgar people… the weak, base, and foolish things of the world’ (compare 1 Corinthians 1:27).

The shared, dominant note is that the later labourers are hired on no bargain but the householder’s justice, and that this trust — rather than the self-interested calculation of the hireling — is the very temper the parable commends. Grace, not contract, governs the kingdom; and those who come at the call without haggling are nearest its spirit. Robertson parses ‘whatsoever is right’ as an indefinite relative with the subjunctive — ‘is fair,’ a just proportionate wage, not the householder’s whim — and the witnesses agree that the latecomers’ silence about terms, set against the disciples’ ‘What shall we have?’, is the very temper of trust the parable holds up for imitation.
5:
‘Again he went out about the sixth and ninth hour, and did likewise.’ Only two voices comment, both briefly, and so the synthesis is short; yet they agree on the basic reckoning and on the rhythm of the householder’s repeated calls.

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke supplies the bare timekeeping: the sixth hour was twelve o’clock, the ninth hour three in the afternoon — the fixed points of the working day at which the householder went out yet again to seek labourers.

The Reformed Gill adds a devotional and typological observation: these three named seasons — the third, sixth, and ninth hours — were also the Jewish hours of prayer, and the householder, ‘seeing others in the same place, and posture… called them, and sent them into his vineyard, to labour there, giving them the same promise he did to others.’ The repeated journeys, on Gill’s reading, display the persistence of grace, which keeps going forth to call at every stage.

With so little comment, the verse mainly carries forward the structure of the parable. The witnesses agree that the householder’s threefold further going-out — at noon, in mid-afternoon, and (as the next verse will add) at the eleventh hour — underlines that the call into the vineyard is not given once for all at daybreak but is renewed throughout the day. Whether the hours be read, with the fathers, as the ages of the world or the stages of human life, or simply as the recurring summons of the gospel, the point that stands is the untiring initiative of the householder, who will not leave willing labourers standing idle while there is work to be done and a wage to be given. The same promise — ‘whatsoever is right’ (v.4) — attends each fresh call. The witnesses agree that the householder’s repeated going-out at noon and mid-afternoon, with the same promise to each, pictures a grace untiring in its search; whether the hours be read as the ages of the world or the stages of a life, the point that stands is that no willing labourer is left unsought while the day lasts and the wage waits to be given.
6:
‘About the eleventh hour he found others standing idle… Why stand ye here all the day idle?’ The witnesses, grammatical-historical with the Reformed Gill, weigh the lateness of the hour and the meaning of the eleventh-hour call.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the time and probe the idleness. Barnes, Clarke and Ellicott agree the eleventh hour was about five in the afternoon, with only one working hour left before the day closed at six (compare John 11:9). Robertson, parsing the accusative of time, names this bluntly ‘the problem of the unemployed,’ the word for idle being ‘no work.’ Ellicott detects a sharper edge: there was still urgent work to be done, the first labourers ‘were not enough,’ and perhaps ‘not labouring as zealously as they might,’ working merely on contract, while those called last had the joy of feeling their day was not lost.

The Reformed Gill reads the hours typologically as either the ages of the world (before the law, under the law, the Messiah’s times, the last days), or the dispensations of the gospel, or the stages of human life — childhood, youth, manhood, and old age — the eleventh hour standing for those called in old age and, ultimately, the Gentiles (compare Hebrews 6:12). Wesley adds simply that the eleventh hour means ‘very late, long after the rest were called.’

The shared, dominant note is that the householder’s grace reaches even to the day’s last hour: none who are willing are turned away for coming late, and the eleventh-hour labourers — whether the aged, or the Gentiles, or any called near the end — are still received into the vineyard. The verse thus prepares for the astonishing equity of the payment, where a single hour’s work, done in trust, will be crowned with the full reward (compare v.9). The witnesses agree that the lateness only magnifies the householder’s generosity: there is still urgent work, and willing hands, at the eleventh hour, and the wage is not forfeited by the hour of the call; the question ‘why stand ye here idle?’ is less rebuke than summons, drawing the last labourers in while a moment of the day remains.
7:
‘Because no man hath hired us… Go ye also into the vineyard.’ The few witnesses, grammatical-historical with the Reformed Gill, read the latecomers’ excuse as genuine and their obedience as prompt.

The grammatical-historical commentators take the excuse at face value. Ellicott calls this ‘one of the salient points of the parable’: the last-called labourers ‘had not rejected any previous summons, and when called they obeyed with alacrity… in full unquestioning faith.’ Barnes cross-refers to the urgency of working while opportunity lasts (John 9:4, Ecclesiastes 9:10). Clarke notes that ‘no man hath hired us’ was the very reason they had stood idle, and observes that the clause ‘whatsoever is right, that shall ye receive’ is wanting in some of the best manuscripts.

The Reformed Gill applies the excuse pointedly to the Gentiles, who for ‘hundreds of years were neglected by God’ — he ‘overlooked the times of their ignorance’ and ‘sent no prophets to instruct them’ — until, the Jews having rejected the Messiah and his apostles, the gospel was ordered to the nations. The latecomers’ plea, on this reading, is the true condition of the Gentile world before the gospel came.

The shared, dominant note is that the idleness of the last labourers was no fault of their own — they had simply not been called — and that they came the moment they were summoned. This both heightens the equity of the householder, who will reward willing latecomers in full, and rebukes any reading that would make the eleventh-hour men careless or unworthy. Whether the plea pictures the Gentiles (Gill) or simply all who are called late, the point that stands is that the householder’s call, not the labourer’s merit, sets the work going; and where the call comes late, the fault lies not with the willing but with the absence of any to hire them. The witnesses agree the latecomers’ plea lays the blame not on their sloth but on the want of any to hire them, and that their prompt obedience, asking no terms, is the very faith the householder rewards in full; where the call comes late, the fault lies not with the willing but with the absence of any to summon them.
8:
‘When even was come… beginning from the last unto the first.’ The witnesses fasten on the order of payment and on the just custom of paying the labourer at day’s end; Calvin warns against over-reading the sequence.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the scene. Barnes describes the steward as the trusted servant who managed the household and paid the workers, and notes that the order — last to first — was immaterial to justice but essential to the parable, for ‘had those first hired been first paid, they would have departed satisfied, and the point… would have been lost.’ Lightfoot and Ellicott ground the prompt payment in the humane Mosaic law that the day-labourer must be paid the same day (Deuteronomy 24:15); the householder’s keeping of it is ‘a feature in his character.’ Ellicott adds that the order illustrates the saying that ‘the last should be first.’

The Reformed Calvin checks the allegorisers: ‘it would be improper to look for a mystery in the injunction’ as if God crowned first those last in time, ‘for such a notion would not at all agree with the doctrine of Paul’ (1 Thessalonians 4:15). The order serves only to make the murmuring possible and so to teach that men have ‘no right to complain of the bounty of God’ when he honours the unworthy with rewards beyond their deserving; Calvin denies the verse is aimed at the envious Jews.

The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note attached here in fact concerns Peter and the keys (Matthew 16:18), and so does not bear on the verse. The shared, dominant note is that the payment is just and prompt, and that the reversed order is a narrative device to expose the grudging heart — not a doctrine that the temporally last are crowned above the first. The witnesses agree that the steward’s paying the last first, though immaterial to justice, is the hinge of the parable, for it lets the first see the latecomers’ wage and so occasions the murmur; the prompt payment, by the humane Mosaic law, marks the householder as just, and the reversed order serves the lesson, not a doctrine of temporal precedence reversed.
9:
‘They that were hired about the eleventh hour… received every man a penny.’ The witnesses agree that the full wage to the one-hour men is the parable’s great surprise and the measure of the householder’s generosity.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the equity-as-generosity. Barnes notes there was ‘no agreement how much they should receive, but merely that justice should be done’ (v.4) and that the householder either reckoned they had earned it or chose ‘to make a present… for the loss of the first part of the day.’ Ellicott presses that the idleness was no fault of theirs, and their readiness at the eleventh hour implied they would have come as readily at daybreak; so ‘the standard of payment was qualitative, not quantitative,’ and the penny, as before, represents the eternal life of the kingdom — ‘no true labourer could receive less; the longest life of labour could claim no more’ (compare Luke 23:40-43).

The Reformed Gill reads the eleventh-hour men as those called in old age, or as the Gentiles, or as the last of the elect called at the end of the world — receiving, by free grace, the same inheritance as the first.

The Wesleyan Wesley holds both senses open: the eleventh-hour labourers are ‘either the Gentiles, who were called long after the Jews… or those in every age who did not hear, or at least understand, the Gospel call, till their day of life was drawing to a period.’ The shared, dominant note is that the householder gives the latecomers a full day’s wage for a fraction of a day’s work — not by injustice, but by a bounty that measures reward by grace rather than by hours, so that the eternal life given to the first is given undiminished to the last. The witnesses agree that the full penny to the one-hour men is the parable’s great surprise and the measure of grace: the householder pays not by the clock but by his bounty, so that the latecomers — whether the aged, the Gentiles, or the last of the elect — receive the whole reward of eternal life, undiminished, that the first received.
10:
‘When the first came, they supposed that they should have received more.’ The witnesses read the first labourers’ false expectation as the seedbed of their grumbling, and several see in it the Jews’ expectation of precedence.

The grammatical-historical commentators trace the disappointed hope. Barnes notes they expected payment ‘not according to contract, but according to the time of the labour.’ Robertson, with Bruce’s wit, asks whether the householder was a humorist in paying each the agreed denarius: ‘false hopes had been raised in those who came first.’ Ellicott turns the lens on the disciples: now, ‘unless they were altogether blind, they must have seen their own thoughts reflected in the parable’ — they too, as their question showed (Matthew 19:27), had expected to receive more, thinking eternal life not enough without ‘some special prerogative.’ Yet the paying of their wage ‘gives a touch of gentleness,’ as with the elder son who was told all the father had was his (Luke 15:31).

The Reformed Gill and the Wesleyan Wesley apply it to the Jews: Gill reads the first as ‘the first saints… or the Jews that first believed,’ and Wesley says plainly ‘the first here may mean the Jews, who supposed they should always be preferred before the Gentiles.’

The shared, dominant note is that the murmuring sprang not from any real wrong but from a proud miscalculation: the first labourers measured their due by comparison with others rather than by the contract they had freely made. Whether the first stand for the Jews jealous of the Gentiles, or for any who serve God in the spirit of merit, the parable exposes the temper that begrudges grace to latecomers — a temper the disciples themselves were in danger of, and which the householder’s gentleness toward even the murmurers gently rebukes. The witnesses agree the first labourers’ disappointment sprang from measuring their due by the latecomers’ wage rather than by their own contract; the parable thus exposes the spirit of merit that begrudges grace, a spirit the disciples themselves, lately asking ‘what shall we have?’, were in danger of sharing.
11:
‘They murmured against the goodman of the house.’ The witnesses agree the grumbling is the parable’s turning-point, and most read it as the Jews’ resentment of the Gentiles’ admission.

The grammatical-historical commentators handle word and sense. Barnes and Ellicott both correct the rendering ‘goodman of the house’ to ‘householder,’ the same word as in v.1, an ‘archaic English phrase’ needlessly varied. Robertson notes the verb is onomatopoetic — ‘murmur and grumble are similar’ — and probably an inchoative imperfect, ‘began to grumble,’ found in old Ionic and the papyri. Ellicott marks the Greek tense as ‘repeated and prolonged murmurs.’

The Wesleyan Clarke gives the historical application: the Jews ‘made the preaching of the Gospel to the Gentiles a pretense why they should reject that Gospel,’ imagining themselves the sole objects of the divine approbation, and he points to their murmuring in Acts 11:1 and Acts 15:1. He notes that many such similitudes circulated among the Jews, but that the Lord ‘greatly improved’ them. Gill reads the murmurers as receiving only ‘the external privileges of the Gospel dispensation.’

The shared, dominant note is that the murmuring exposes a heart that begrudges the householder’s generosity to others. Whether the grumblers picture the Jews offended at the call of the Gentiles, or any servant who resents grace shown to latecomers, the parable lays bare the envy that turns even into a complaint against God — the very disposition that v.15 will name as the ‘evil eye.’ The next verse gives their complaint its sharpest voice. Barnes and Ellicott note that the murmuring is directed against the very householder whose generosity they had benefited from, and that the complaint, as the next verse shows, is not that they were underpaid but that others were made their equals. The witnesses agree this resentment of grace shown to others is the heart of the ‘evil eye’ the householder will name, whether the grumblers picture the Jews offended at the call of the Gentiles or any servant who serves in the spirit of merit. The next verse will give their grudging complaint its sharpest and most plausible voice, which the householder must then answer.
12:
‘These last have wrought but one hour, and thou hast made them equal unto us, which have borne the burden and heat of the day.’ The witnesses dwell on the labourers’ plausible-seeming grievance and, most of them, on its application to the Jews.

The grammatical-historical commentators bring the scene to life. Barnes explains the ‘burden and heat of the day’ as the severe toil borne through the heat, while the latecomers worked ‘in the cool of the evening.’ Ellicott and Robertson identify the ‘heat’ with the kauson, the scorching east wind or sirocco that ‘blasted the grain in Pharaoh’s dream’ (Genesis 41:6), ‘withered Jonah’s gourd’ (Jonah 4:8), and blighted the vine in Ezekiel’s parable (Ezekiel 17:10); Robertson grants the sweat-stained men ‘seemed to have a good case.’

The Reformed Gill and the Wesleyan Wesley press the application to the Jews. Gill notes the grievance that one hour’s service should be paid as many hours’, ‘grudged by the Jews.’ Wesley turns it directly to Acts 15:9: ‘Thou hast made them equal to us’ — God ‘hath put no difference between us (Jews) and them (Gentiles), purifying their hearts by faith’; and those ‘equally holy here… will be equally happy hereafter.’

The shared, dominant note is that the complaint, however reasonable it sounds, rests on the false premise that reward is owed by the hour rather than given by grace. The labourers do not charge the householder with paying them less than agreed, but with making the latecomers their equals; and this resentment of equality — whether the Jews’ toward the Gentiles, or any servant’s toward those called late — is the very envy the householder will answer in the verses that follow, where the bargain itself (v.13) silences every claim of wrong. The witnesses agree the complaint, however reasonable in the mouth of a day-labourer, rests on the false premise that reward is owed by the hour rather than given by grace; the grievance is not underpayment but equality, and this resentment of the householder’s generosity to others is precisely the temper his reply will rebuke.
13:
‘Friend, I do thee no wrong: didst not thou agree with me for a penny?’ The witnesses agree the householder’s reply rests on strict justice, and several mark the reproof carried in the word ‘friend.’

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the address. Ellicott and Robertson note that ‘friend’ (literally comrade) ‘always carries with it in our Lord’s lips a tone of reproof,’ used also to the man without a wedding garment (Matthew 22:12) and to Judas (Matthew 26:50). Barnes draws the lesson that the householder, having fully met the contract, may do as he pleases with the rest — so Christians who are just and pay their debts ‘have a right to do with it as they please,’ giving to the poor or to missions. Ellicott adds that, had the first labourers shared the householder’s generosity, ‘they would not have grudged the others’ but found their reward in sympathy, as no disciple entered into his Master’s spirit would grudge the penitent thief his rest in Paradise (Luke 23:43).

The Wesleyan Clarke applies it to the call of the Gentiles, which ‘can in itself become no impediment to the Jews… there is room enough in heaven for all.’

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem is a catechetical meditation on the penitent thief and the swiftness of grace, not a comment on this verse, though it touches the same theme of mercy to the latecomer. The shared, dominant note is that no labourer has any ground of complaint: the householder has kept his word to the letter, and his liberality to others takes nothing from those who received exactly what they bargained for. Justice is done to all; the murmur arises only from an eye that grudges the goodness shown to another. The witnesses agree the householder’s appeal to the bargain silences every charge of wrong: he has paid the first exactly what they agreed, and his liberality to the last takes nothing from them; the word ‘friend’ carries its gentle reproof, and the whole answer turns the murmurer back upon his own consent, where justice, not grievance, is found.
14:
‘Take that thine is, and go thy way: I will give unto this last, even as unto thee.’ The witnesses read the householder’s words as both a dismissal and a declaration of his sovereign generosity; one wrestles with what the dismissal means for the murmurer.

The grammatical-historical commentators handle tone and difficulty. Barnes reads it simply: ‘Take what is justly due to you — what is properly your own’ (compare John 17:2). Robertson catches the manner: ‘pick it up,’ as if the man had ‘contemptuously thrown the denarius on the ground,’ and locates the point of the parable in the householder’s sovereign ‘what I will… in the sphere of my own affairs.’ Ellicott raises the harder question — whether the murmuring labourers, though paid, are by their discontent ‘excluded… from all share’ in the reward — and answers that the reward ‘lies in the presence of God to the soul,’ whose blessedness depends on harmony with the mind of God, so that heaven ‘is not a place, but a state.’

The Reformed Gill reads the dismissal as true of ‘many among the Jews, who were only nominal professors, and from whom the Gospel… was taken.’ The Wesleyan Wesley hears the householder’s sovereign will to give ‘to this last called among the heathens even as to the first called among the Jews,’ and even to ‘the late converted publicans and sinners.’

The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here in fact treats taking up the cross (Mark 8:34) and does not bear on the verse. The shared, dominant note is that the householder asserts an unanswerable right: he gives the first their just due and the last his free bounty, and the murmurer is sent away with exactly what he agreed to — the reward of grace being his to bestow as he pleases. The witnesses agree the householder asserts an unanswerable right: he gives the first their just due and the last his free bounty, dismissing the murmurer with exactly what he agreed to; the reward of grace, all concur, is the householder’s to bestow as he pleases, and the dismissal warns that a grudging heart may keep its wage yet miss the spirit of the kingdom.
15:
‘Is it not lawful for me to do what I will with mine own? Is thine eye evil, because I am good?’ The witnesses converge on two great themes: the sovereignty of the householder and the nature of envy.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound the ‘evil eye.’ Barnes, Ellicott and Robertson agree the Hebrew idiom denotes the envious and malicious (Deuteronomy 15:9, Proverbs 23:6), ‘displeased at the prosperity of others’ (compare Mark 7:22); Robertson contrasts the grudging eye with the householder’s generous one. Ellicott stresses the question ‘appeals tacitly to a standard which none could question’: as far as the labourer was concerned, the householder had a right to give freely of his own, being ‘responsible to God only.’ Clarke draws out, at length, the allegory of the Church as a vineyard and the calls at successive hours.

The Reformed Gill reads the ‘own’ as the external gifts, the special grace, and the heaven that are God’s to bestow on whom he will — chosen, adopted, justified, and glorified ‘according to his sovereign good will and pleasure’ (compare Romans 9:15-24). The Wesleyan Wesley, however, sets a firm limit: from the householder’s right to give the undeserving more than they merit, it cannot be inferred that God may ‘consign an unborn soul to hell’; the sovereignty is a sovereignty of bounty, and ‘is thine eye evil because I am good?’ rebukes a selfish and envious temper.

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem is a catechetical passage on the penitent thief, tangential to the verse. The shared, dominant note is that God’s freedom to be generous wrongs no one, and that to begrudge his goodness to others is the ‘evil eye’ — a sin, as Henry will add, that ‘has neither pleasure, profit, nor honour in it.’ The witnesses agree that God’s freedom to be generous wrongs no one, and that to begrudge his goodness to others is the very ‘evil eye’ the householder names; whether the sovereignty be pressed, with Gill, to the free bestowal of grace and glory, or limited, with Wesley, to bounty alone, all concur that envy of grace is a sin that has, as Henry said, neither pleasure, profit, nor honour in it.
16:
‘So the last shall be first, and the first last: for many be called, but few chosen.’ The witnesses agree this is the parable’s moral, but divide sharply over its precise reach.

The grammatical-historical commentators narrow it. Barnes argues the parable ‘teaches one truth, and but one’: that within the church Christ makes a difference, raising some (Paul, Martyn, Brainerd) to wider usefulness and higher reward ‘without regard to the time which they serve’ — it has ‘no reference to the call of the Gentiles… nor to the doctrine of election.’ Robertson notes the adjectives change places from Matthew 19:30, suiting the parable. Ellicott reads it of degrees in the life to come, depending ‘not on the duration of service… but on the temper and character of the worker,’ with a latent reference to the rich young ruler; he and Clarke both note that ‘many… few chosen’ is absent from the best manuscripts here (it stands in Matthew 22:14).

The Reformed Calvin likewise restrains it: Christ does not here compare Jew and Gentile or reprobate and elect, but only urges that whoever is called before others should ‘run with so much the greater alacrity’ and share the common prize modestly, since God ‘calls freely whomsoever He pleases.’ The Wesleyan Clarke and Wesley keep the Jew/Gentile sense, Clarke illustrating the ‘many called, few chosen’ by the Roman custom of selecting soldiers; Wesley glosses ‘called’ as all who hear the gospel, ‘chosen’ as only those who obey it.

The shared, dominant note is that the parable overturns all reckoning by seniority or merit: reward goes by grace and by the worker’s temper, not by the hour of his calling. The schools differ chiefly on scope — degrees of reward within the church (Barnes), the call of the Gentiles (Clarke, Wesley), or simply the duty of humble diligence (Calvin) — but all agree that none may presume on being first. The witnesses agree that, whatever its precise scope, the saying overturns all reckoning by seniority: reward goes by grace and by the worker’s temper, never by the mere hour of his calling, so that none may presume on being first nor despair of being last.
17:
Jesus, going up to Jerusalem, takes the twelve apart to foretell his passion. The witnesses agree this is the third and fullest prediction of the cross, and the fathers especially mark the privacy and the willingness of Christ.

The Greek and Latin fathers probe the manner. Chrysostom (with the separate Chrysostom entry) explains why the disclosure is private: ‘it was not meet that his discourse about these things should be published to the many,’ for if the disciples were confounded, much more the multitude; he stresses that Jesus comes to his passion ‘wittingly and willingly.’ Pseudo-Chrysostom adds that ‘the more precious treasure is ever committed to the more worthy vessels,’ and that to be willing to die ‘is more than human.’ Jerome and Raban note he fortifies them against the scandal of the cross, Raban tracing the chain — Judas to the Jews, the Jews to the Gentiles. Augustine draws from the two deaths and one resurrection a meditation on the number three.

The grammatical-historical commentators and the Reformed Calvin add the setting and the purpose. Barnes and Ellicott place the journey after the raising of Lazarus and the sojourn at Ephraim (John 11:54), and note from Mark that Jesus ‘went before them’ while they ‘were amazed… and afraid’ (Mark 10:32) — the shadow of the cross already upon him. Calvin says he repeats the prediction to ‘exhort them to steadiness,’ fortifying them by foreknowledge and by the proof of his divinity, and (with Luke) by appeal to the prophets.

The Puritan Henry adds the pastoral grain: it is ‘good for us to be often thinking and speaking of our death,’ a way of ‘dying daily.’ The shared, dominant note is that Christ, with his heart set on his suffering-work, privately steels the Twelve for what is coming. The witnesses agree that Christ, his heart set on his suffering-work, privately steels the Twelve for what is coming, repeating the prediction in fuller detail than before; the privacy spares the multitude, the willingness attests his love, and the resurrection, here as elsewhere, is joined to the cross as its appointed sequel and the disciples’ comfort.
18:
‘Behold, we go up to Jerusalem; and the Son of man shall be betrayed unto the chief priests and scribes, and they shall condemn him to death.’ The witnesses stress the precision of the prophecy and the willingness it reveals.

The grammatical-historical commentators detail the prediction. Barnes lays out the stages: betrayed (Matthew 26:15), condemned by the Sanhedrin who lacked power to execute (Matthew 26:66), and so delivered to the Gentiles — all foretold in Isaiah 53 and Daniel 9:26. Ellicott notes the new definiteness over Matthew 17:22: Jerusalem the scene, the chief priests and scribes the actors, the Gentiles their instruments, with the mocking, spitting and scourging now ‘new elements’ brought ‘vividly… before His mind and theirs.’

The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrine: Christ ‘was endued with divine fortitude for overcoming the terrors of death, for he knowingly and willingly hastens to undergo it,’ and the minute detail of the circumstances is itself ‘a still more evident proof of his Divinity,’ for none but God could so foresee them. Yet he does not spare the disciples the ‘very grievous offense’ of seeing the whole sacred order opposed to Christ, but candidly declares it and points to his resurrection as the way to overcome the temptation.

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem is on point here, urging that Christ ‘came to His passion willingly… lest the whole world should perish in its sins,’ and that ‘the Cross is a glory to Jesus.’ The shared, dominant note is that Christ foresees and freely embraces a death foretold in every particular — the betrayal, the unjust condemnation, the handing over to the Gentiles — so that his foreknowledge attests his Godhead and his willingness his love. The witnesses agree that Christ foresees and freely embraces a death foretold in every particular — the betrayal, the unjust condemnation by the Sanhedrin, the handing over to the Gentiles — so that his minute foreknowledge attests his Godhead and his willingness his love; and that he does not hide from the disciples the grievous offense of seeing the whole sacred order arrayed against him, but points them beyond it to the resurrection.
19:
‘And shall deliver him to the Gentiles to mock, and to scourge, and to crucify him: and the third day he shall rise again.’ The witnesses note the Gentile instruments, the manner of death, and the resurrection the disciples could not grasp.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the agents and the prophecies. Gill and Clarke specify that the delivery to the Gentiles meant Pilate and the Roman soldiers — the mocking by Herod and his soldiers (Luke 23:11), the scourging and crucifixion by Pilate — crucifixion being a Roman, not a Jewish, punishment. Barnes ties the scourging to Isaiah 53:5 and the delivery to Acts 3:13. Robertson observes that ‘the details fall on deaf ears, even the point of the resurrection.’ Clarke marvels that they were unwittingly offering ‘that sacrifice which was to make an atonement… for the whole world.’

Ellicott dwells on the resurrection clause: it ‘came as a sequel… The Master looked beyond the suffering to the victory over death, but the disciples could not enter into the meaning,’ for, as Luke reports (Luke 18:34), ‘they understood none of these things… this saying was hid from them.’ All was to them ‘a dark and dim dream.’

The shared, dominant note is that Christ’s prediction reaches its climax in both the shame of the cross — mockery, scourging, crucifixion at Gentile hands — and the triumph of the third day. Jew and Gentile together would have a hand in his death, for he was to suffer for the salvation of both; and the resurrection, plainly foretold, is set as the answer to the cross, though its comfort was lost on disciples whose eyes were still holden. The wrath of man, Clarke adds, would only praise God (compare Psalms 76:10). The witnesses agree the prediction climbs to its climax in both the shame of the cross — mockery, scourging, crucifixion at Gentile hands — and the triumph of the third day; Jew and Gentile alike would share in his death, for he was to suffer for the salvation of both, and the plainly foretold resurrection stands as the answer to the cross, though its comfort was hidden from disciples whose eyes were still holden.
20:
The mother of Zebedee’s sons comes worshipping, to ask a favour. The witnesses agree the request, coming just after the passion prediction, betrays an earthly view of the kingdom; they divide on whether the mother or the sons were the true petitioners.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene. Barnes, Ellicott and Robertson identify the mother as Salome (Matthew 27:56, Mark 15:40), possibly a sister of the Lord’s mother (John 19:25), prompting her sons because of the kinship. Robertson calls the timing ‘surely an inopportune’ one, their minds preoccupied with the twelve thrones of Matthew 19:28. Ellicott notes she shared the hope that ‘the kingdom of God should immediately appear’ (Luke 19:11).

The Latin and Greek fathers moralise. Jerome blames her ‘womanish eagerness’ that grasps the present and forgets the future. Pseudo-Chrysostom praises Salome as ‘truly peaceful’ and her sons as seeking ‘not earthly things, but heavenly,’ yet faults the request as ‘inconsiderate’ and reads ‘Ye know not what ye ask’ as a rebuke of ignorance, not malice. Origen sees ‘womanish simplicity’ and the brothers’ imperfect notions of the kingdom. Augustine harmonises with Mark, where the sons themselves ask (Mark 10:35).

The Reformed Calvin calls the narrative ‘a bright mirror of human vanity,’ showing how ‘holy zeal is often accompanied by ambition’; the brothers, restrained by bashfulness, put their mother forward, their conscience reproving them. The Puritan Henry notes that ‘whom Christ loves best he reproves most.’ The shared, dominant note is that a real faith in Christ’s coming kingdom was corrupted by a carnal ambition for the highest places. The witnesses agree that the request, coming hard upon the prediction of the cross, betrays how little the disciples had grasped their Master’s words, their minds fixed on thrones rather than suffering. Whether Salome spoke for her sons or they through her, all concur that a genuine faith in Christ’s coming kingdom was corrupted by a carnal ambition for the highest places — a vanity Christ would meet not with a harsh rebuke but with a patient unveiling of the law of service.
21:
‘Grant that these my two sons may sit, the one on thy right hand, and the other on the left, in thy kingdom.’ The witnesses agree the request mingles a true faith in Christ’s reign with a carnal mistake about its nature.

The grammatical-historical commentators expose the error. Barnes says they were ‘still looking for a temporal kingdom’ with ‘pomp and glory,’ seeking the places of highest honour at a prince’s right and left (1 Kings 2:19). Clarke connects the request with the promise of the twelve thrones (Matthew 19:28) taken literally, and notes the rabbinic arrangement of the Sanhedrin’s president flanked by the ‘father’ and the ‘sage,’ suggesting they sought ecclesiastical rather than secular honours. Ellicott observes the favour already shown — John ‘the disciple whom Jesus loved’ (John 13:23), James one of the chosen three — might have seemed to warrant the petition.

The Reformed Calvin balances praise and blame: it was ‘a noble specimen of faith’ to expect a kingdom when Christ appeared ‘under the mean aspect of a servant’; yet ‘how easily the pure seed… becomes degenerate,’ for they imagined a kingdom that ‘had no existence’ and coveted the highest places. He draws the prayer that God would keep our faith ‘pure from all mixture.’ Gill notes Christ’s humanity, goodness and patience in not upbraiding their pride.

The Wesleyan Wesley adds tersely that ‘still they expected a temporal kingdom.’ The shared, dominant note is that the brothers’ faith in Christ’s coming glory was real and even commendable, but corrupted by ambition and by a mistaken, earthly picture of the reign in which they sought precedence. The witnesses agree the brothers’ confidence in Christ’s reign was, in itself, a noble faith — for they looked for a kingdom while he appeared under the mean aspect of a servant — yet it was marred by ambition and by an earthly picture of that reign in which they coveted precedence over the rest. All concur that the seeking of the highest places, just after the Lord had spoken of his cross, shows how easily the pure seed of faith degenerates, and how needful it is to pray that our faith be kept pure from the mixture of self-seeking.
22:
‘Ye know not what ye ask. Are ye able to drink of the cup that I shall drink of?’ The witnesses agree the cup (and baptism) is suffering and martyrdom, and that the brothers’ confident ‘We are able’ betrays their ignorance.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound the imagery and the text. Barnes and Ellicott show that ‘to drink of a cup’ means to be afflicted (Isaiah 51:17, Psalms 75:8), and the baptism to be overwhelmed in floods of calamity (Psalms 42:7, Luke 12:50). Lightfoot notes that Jewish baptism, a total plunging, fitly signified ‘the most cruel kind of death.’ Clarke and Ellicott both observe that the clause ‘and to be baptized with the baptism’ is absent from many of the best manuscripts, probably added from Mark. Robertson notes ‘ask’ is indirect middle — ‘a selfish request’ — and their ‘We are able’ an ‘amazing proof of their ignorance… they had not caught the martyr spirit.’

The Reformed Calvin holds Christ ‘corrects their ambition… by holding out the cross,’ teaching that none will share his kingdom ‘who has not previously shared in his sufferings and death’; their bold answer ‘manifests the presumption of the flesh.’ The Wesleyan Clarke turns it on those who covet church office, and Wesley says all who share his kingdom ‘must first share in his sufferings.’

The Catena entry under Cyril of Jerusalem is on point, naming martyrdom a baptism ‘in their own blood.’ The shared, dominant note is that the path to glory runs through suffering, and that the brothers, ignorant of what they pledged, are led to count the cost. The witnesses agree the cup and baptism denote suffering and martyrdom, and that the brothers’ ready ‘We are able’ betrays a presumption born of ignorance, for they had not yet caught the martyr spirit. All concur that Christ corrects their ambition not by denying them honour but by holding out the cross, teaching that none shares his kingdom who has not first shared his sufferings; the path to glory runs, for the disciple as for the Master, through the cup of affliction freely drunk.
23:
‘Ye shall drink indeed of my cup… but to sit on my right hand… is not mine to give, but it shall be given to them for whom it is prepared of my Father.’ The witnesses agree on the predicted martyrdom and unite in correcting the misleading translation of the second clause.

The grammatical-historical commentators press both points. Barnes, Clarke and Ellicott all object that the italicised words ‘it shall be given’ pervert the sense, making Christ deny that he bestows rewards — contrary to John 5:22; the true rendering is ‘is not mine to give, except to those for whom it is prepared of my Father’ (the same phrase recurs in Matthew 25:34). As prediction, they note the contrast in the brothers’ fates: James the first apostle martyred (Acts 12:2), John surviving to old age, exile, and the struggle of Revelation 1:9.

The Reformed Calvin explains that Christ ‘surrenders nothing,’ but states that the Father had not assigned to him ‘this office of appointing to each person his own peculiar place’; he reasons not about his power but about his calling, and warns us ‘to be wise with sobriety’ and not pry into the future state (1 John 3:2). The Wesleyan Wesley agrees the place ‘is his to give in the strictest propriety,’ but only ‘to those for whom it is prepared’ — those ‘who endure to the end in the faith that worketh by love.’

The Catena’s Cyril of Jerusalem entry repeats the martyr-baptism theme. The shared, dominant note is twofold: the brothers shall indeed share Christ’s sufferings, and the highest places are no arbitrary gift but are bestowed by the Father’s eternal purpose on those prepared for them. The witnesses are united that the italicised ‘it shall be given’ distorts the verse, as though Christ disowned the bestowal of rewards; rightly read, the highest places are no arbitrary gift but are reserved by the Father’s eternal purpose for those prepared for them. All concur, too, that the brothers shall indeed drink Christ’s cup — James first of the apostles by the sword, John through exile and long tribulation.
24:
‘And when the ten heard it, they were moved with indignation against the two brethren.’ The witnesses agree the ten’s anger was itself a symptom of the same ambition, not a righteous zeal.

The grammatical-historical commentators name the fault. Barnes and Robertson note the strong word for ‘angry resentment’: the ten ‘felt that James and John had taken advantage of their relation to Jesus.’ Ellicott is explicit that it was ‘not a righteous indignation… but rather the jealousy of rivals,’ angry that the two had sought an unfair advantage. Clarke generalises that ‘the ambition which leads to spiritual lordship… has proved the ruin of the most flourishing Churches.’

The Greek and Latin fathers probe the motives. Chrysostom observes that only now, hearing the Lord rebuke the two, did the others vent the grief they had nursed since the Transfiguration, not daring before ‘out of respect to their teacher.’ Pseudo-Chrysostom remarks that ‘as the two had asked carnally, so now the ten are grieved carnally,’ for ‘to have another above us is mortifying to our vanity.’ Origen notes the rulers of this world are ‘severe and oppressive,’ but among Christ’s own it shall not be so.

The Reformed Calvin judges the ten’s displeasure ‘far from being well-founded’: the Lord intended to ‘lay open a disease which was lurking within them,’ for ‘there was not one… who would willingly yield to others.’ The shared, dominant note is that the quarrel exposed a wicked ambition common to all twelve, so that Christ’s coming words on servanthood (vv.26–28) address not two offenders but the whole company. The witnesses agree the ten’s anger was no righteous zeal but the jealousy of rivals, betraying that the same ambition lurked in them all; only now, hearing the Lord’s reply to the two, did they vent the grief they had nursed since the Transfiguration. All concur that the quarrel exposed a disease common to the whole company, so that Christ’s ensuing words on servanthood were aimed not at two offenders but at the twelve, and through them at every disciple tempted to seek the chief place rather than the lowest.
25:
‘The princes of the Gentiles exercise dominion over them… but it shall not be so among you.’ The witnesses agree Christ sets the world’s way of lordship against the servanthood of his kingdom; Calvin and Gill press the bearing on church office.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw the contrast. Barnes says the kings of the earth raise favourites to power, ‘but my kingdom is established in a different manner… no ranks; no places of dominion,’ the most distinguished being the most humble; he glosses ‘minister’ as deacon, a servant. Clarke and Ellicott render the two strong verbs ‘lord it over’ and exercise oppressive authority (compare 1 Peter 5:3), Clarke adding that when ‘political matters are brought into the Church… both are ruined.’

The Reformed Calvin gives the fullest treatment, distinguishing ‘the spiritual government of his Church’ from ‘the empires of the world’: pastors are appointed ‘not to rule but to serve.’ He guards against the Anabaptist error of excluding magistrates from the Church — the comparison is ‘not between Christians and ungodly men, but between the nature of their offices’ — and notes that even kings ‘do not rule justly… unless they serve,’ though their office, unlike the apostolic, admits scepter and throne. Gill likewise limits the saying to apostles and pastors, who have no ‘lordly power’ over one another.

The Wesleyan Wesley puts it tersely: the disciples imagined the chief in his kingdom would lord it as the Gentiles do, ‘but it will be quite otherwise.’ The shared, dominant note is that greatness in Christ’s kingdom is the reverse of worldly dominion — not authority wielded over others, but service rendered to them. The witnesses agree that Christ sets the world’s lordship in sharp contrast to the servanthood of his kingdom: the princes of the Gentiles wield dominion, ‘but it shall not be so among you.’ All concur that greatness in the Church is the reverse of worldly power — not authority exercised over others but service rendered to them — and Calvin and Gill press that pastors are appointed not to rule but to minister, the spiritual government of the Church differing in its very nature from the empires of the world.
26:
‘Whosoever will be great among you, let him be your minister.’ The witnesses agree that Christ does not condemn the desire for greatness but redirects it to service.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw the paradox. Robertson is emphatic that ‘Jesus does not condemn the desire to become great… a laudable ambition’; there are ‘great ones’ among Christians, but they do not ‘lord it over’ others — ‘the way to be first… is to be your servant… a complete reversal of popular opinion.’ Ellicott urges the ambitious disciple ‘at once to satisfy and purify’ his longing ‘by finding his greatness in active service,’ for that service ‘is in itself the truest and highest greatness.’ Barnes points to the deacon’s servant-office (compare Matthew 23:11).

The Reformed Calvin explains that since ‘every man is carried away by a love of himself,’ Christ bids that passion be turned to a new object: ‘let the only greatness… you desire be to submit to your brethren.’ The Wesleyan Clarke draws a fivefold portrait of the true minister — not lording over the flock, not imperious, reforming the weak by loving instruction, serving with all his talents, ready to lay down his life. Gill restrains the saying from abolishing all church order, applying it to the equality of the apostles and pastors among themselves.

The shared, dominant note is that the kingdom inverts the world’s scale: the path to true eminence is downward, through humble and useful service to others, so that the disciple’s very ambition, rightly aimed, becomes the engine of his lowliness. The witnesses agree that Christ does not condemn the desire for greatness but redirects it, making service the path to true eminence — a complete reversal of popular opinion. All concur that the disciple’s very ambition, rightly aimed, becomes the engine of his lowliness, and that the would-be great must find his greatness in active and humble usefulness to his brethren; Calvin adds that the self-love native to every man is here turned to a new object, that the only primacy we covet should be to submit to and serve one another. The disciple who would be first, in short, must learn to descend, finding his eminence in the lowest place of loving usefulness to others.
27:
‘And whosoever will be chief among you, let him be your servant.’ The few witnesses, grammatical-historical with the Reformed Gill, intensify the lesson of the previous verse: the higher the place desired, the lower the service required.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the deepening. Ellicott notes that ‘servant’ (better, slave) ‘implies a lower and more menial service’ than the ‘minister’ of v.26, just as ‘chief’ or ‘first’ is higher than ‘great’; he warns against the ‘false antithesis’ that would assign service to this life and greatness to the next — ‘the greatness is the service.’ Gill glosses ‘chief’ as wishing the first place, the pre-eminence in the Messiah’s kingdom.

The Wesleyan Clarke draws from ‘servant’ (the lowest secular office, often a slave) a portrait of the gospel minister: he is not to consider himself a lord over Christ’s flock, not to act with an imperious spirit, to reform the weak by loving instruction rather than censure, to count apostolic greatness as serving the followers of Christ with all his powers, and to be ready, if required, to lay down his life for their salvation.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are united: the saying repeats and sharpens the great reversal of the kingdom. To be ‘chief’ one must descend to the place of a bond-servant; the loftiest rank Christ recognises is the lowest stoop of love. The disciple who would be first must become the slave of all, finding his primacy, as Calvin said on the foregoing verses, in submitting to his brethren — a primacy measured not by the honour received but by the service rendered, and crowned only by conformity to the Master who himself came not to be served but to serve (v.28). The witnesses agree the saying deepens the lesson: the higher the place desired, the lower the service required, so that to be chief one must become the bond-servant of all. All concur that the loftiest rank Christ recognises is the lowest stoop of love, and that the disciple’s primacy is measured not by honour received but by service rendered, crowned only by conformity to the Master who came not to be served but to serve.
28:
‘Even as the Son of man came not to be ministered unto, but to minister, and to give his life a ransom for many.’ The witnesses agree this is the climactic ground of the call to service — Christ’s own self-abasement — and the chapter’s clearest statement of substitutionary atonement.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound the ransom. Barnes defines it as ‘a price paid for the redemption of captives’: men are captives to sin (Romans 3:9-20), and Christ ‘died in the place of sinners,’ God accepting his death ‘in the place of the eternal suffering of the redeemed’ (1 Peter 1:18-19). Clarke and Ellicott root lutron in the Septuagint’s ransom-price (Exodus 21:30, Leviticus 25:50), Clarke rendering ‘a ransom instead of many’ and noting the whole ancient world believed in vicarious sacrifice. Robertson, against those who would evade it, insists Jesus ‘rises to the full consciousness of the significance of his death,’ the preposition anti carrying ‘the notion of exchange’ — the purchase-money for manumitting slaves.

The Reformed Calvin confirms the doctrine: Christ ‘voluntarily emptied himself’ (Philippians 2:7), and ‘his life is the price of our redemption,’ this single word overturning the ‘idle talk of the Papists’ about merit. He reads ‘many’ not as a fixed number but in contrast to one — Christ set against ‘all the rest of men’ (compare Romans 5:15, 1 Timothy 2:6).

The Catena’s Cyril of Jerusalem entry quotes Daniel’s Son of man (Daniel 7:13). The shared, dominant note is that the Lord of all became the servant of all and gave his life as a substitutionary ransom — the supreme pattern of the humility the disciples were enjoined to learn. The witnesses are united that the Lord of all became the servant of all and gave his life as a substitutionary ransom, a price paid in the sinner’s place, and that this self-abasement is the supreme ground of the humility the disciples were enjoined to learn. All concur that ‘many’ is set in contrast not to a few but to the one who died for all, and that this single word of Christ overturns every scheme of self-purchased merit.
29:
As Jesus departs from Jericho, a great multitude follows and two blind men cry out. The witnesses divide on harmonising the accounts, but unite on the blind men’s persevering faith.

The grammatical-historical commentators tackle the harmony. Barnes and Robertson reconcile Mark’s and Luke’s naming of one man (Bartimaeus) with Matthew’s two — the one being better known — and Luke’s placing the miracle as Jesus ‘came nigh’ Jericho (Luke 18:35), Robertson suggesting old and new Jericho with the men ‘between the two towns.’ Barnes gives a full account of Jericho’s history (Joshua 6:26, 1 Kings 16:34).

The Greek and Latin fathers read both the persistence and the figure. Pseudo-Chrysostom marvels that ‘spiritual love feels no fatigue’ and that the blind, having ‘nothing free about them but their voice… pursued Him with their voice’; ‘faith is quickened by being prohibited.’ Chrysostom notes Christ ‘suffered them to be forbidden, that their desire might be the more evidenced.’ Origen and Augustine allegorise the two blind men as Israel and the Gentiles, or as souls coming by faith to be enlightened, who must cry over the din of the worldly crowd — for ‘bad or lukewarm Christians are a hindrance to good Christians.’ Jerome and Raban take the men for the Pharisees and Sadducees, or read Jericho (‘the moon’) as the changeful world.

The Puritan Henry draws the pastoral lessons: joint prayer is pleasing to Christ (Matthew 18:19), and those who would receive mercy must place themselves where Christ passes. The shared, dominant note is that earnest, persevering faith presses through every discouragement to reach Christ. The witnesses agree the apparent discrepancies between the Gospels — one blind man or two, entering Jericho or leaving it — yield to patient harmony and in no way diminish the men’s persevering faith. All concur that earnest desire presses through every obstacle to reach Christ, the blind pursuing him with their voice when they could not with their feet; and the fathers add the figure of souls coming by faith to be enlightened, who must cry over the din of a worldly crowd that would silence them.
30:
‘Two blind men… cried out, Have mercy on us, O Lord, thou Son of David.’ The witnesses dwell on the harmonising question and on the faith expressed in the title ‘Son of David.’

The grammatical-historical commentators address the number. Barnes, Ellicott and Robertson again explain that Mark and Luke mention only the conspicuous Bartimaeus; Gill proposes ‘three blind men’ (one before, two after Jericho); Ellicott candidly allows that Luke, inquiring on the spot, ‘may have made his narrative more accurate.’ They note the men echoed the crowd’s whispered identification, using, like the woman of Canaan (Matthew 15:22), the most popular messianic title and appealing ‘to the pity of the King.’

The Reformed Calvin and the Alexandrian Origen weigh the confession. Calvin says they ‘confer on Christ no ordinary honor’ in seeking relief from him, and their faith ‘is still more clearly exhibited’ in acknowledging him Messiah, the ‘Son of David’ — not ‘some Prophet,’ but the ‘only Author of salvation.’ Origen gathers the various titles given Christ — ‘Son of David’ by these and the Canaanite, ‘Son of God’ by others, with or without the ‘truly’ (v.33) — to show ‘the difference of those who come’ to him.

The Wesleyan Wesley notes Bartimaeus was ‘far the more eminent… and spoke for both.’ The shared, dominant note is that the blind men, naming Christ ‘Son of David,’ confess the Messiah and cast themselves on his mercy — a faith the apparent discrepancies of the Gospels in no way diminish. Barnes, Ellicott and Robertson agree that Mark and Luke name only the conspicuous Bartimaeus while Matthew counts two, and that such variation, far from contradiction, marks the honest independence of the witnesses. All concur that the blind men, naming Christ ‘Son of David,’ confess the promised Messiah and cast themselves on his mercy; their cry is no mere request for alms but an appeal to the King, grounded in the conviction that he is able to save. Their faith, all the witnesses agree, the apparent discrepancies of the Gospel records in no way diminish, but rather confirm by the very independence of the accounts.
31:
‘The multitude rebuked them… but they cried the more.’ The witnesses agree the crowd’s rebuke only intensified the blind men’s faith, and draw from it a lesson on perseverance in prayer.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe the rebuke. Gill suggests the multitude, whether friends or enemies, chid them as troublesome or were displeased at the ‘high characters’ — Lord, Son of David — they gave Christ. Barnes adds from Mark that Jesus stood still and ordered them called, and that Bartimaeus ‘cast away his garment’ in haste to come. Ellicott observes the litanies of Christendom have for centuries been ‘modelled on the Kyrie Eleïson’ that came from their lips.

The Wesleyan Clarke turns it to the soul: ‘whenever a soul begins to cry after Jesus… the world and the devil join together to drown its cries’; let such ‘cry a great deal the more,’ for the rebuke is ‘a proof that the salvation of God is nigh.’ Wesley likewise: those who begin to cry after the Son of David will be charged to hold their peace, but ‘let those who feel their need of him cry the more; otherwise they will come short of a cure.’

The Reformed Calvin finds it ‘surprising that the disciples of Christ… should wish to drive wretched men from the favor of Christ,’ and warns that often ‘the greater part of those who profess the name of Christ… rather hinder or delay our approach’; perseverance is therefore necessary, the redoubled cry overcoming every obstacle Satan throws in the way. The shared, dominant note is that opposition, far from silencing true faith, drives it to cry the louder until Christ is reached. The witnesses agree that the crowd’s rebuke, far from silencing the blind men, drove them to cry the louder, and that this perseverance is the very temper of true prayer. All concur that opposition — even from those who profess to follow Christ — is to be expected and overcome by redoubled earnestness; Calvin warns that the greater part of professors often hinder rather than help our approach, while Clarke and Wesley find in the rebuke itself a proof that salvation is near.
32:
‘And Jesus stood still, and called them, and said, What will ye that I shall do unto you?’ The witnesses dwell on Christ’s stopping for the blind, and on his asking what he already knew.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the manner. Gill says the standing still ‘shows the strength of faith, the force of prayer, and the great regard Christ has to both.’ Ellicott gives, from Mark, the very message — ‘Be of good cheer, arise; He calleth thee’ — and Bartimaeus flinging off his cloak to leap up and come; the question is asked ‘with the clear insight of sympathy.’ Clarke exclaims that ‘the cry of a believing penitent… is sufficient to stop the most merciful Jesus,’ for one immortal soul outweighs all the irrational creation.

The Reformed Calvin explains why Christ asks a seemingly needless question: ‘he had determined to grant their requests,’ and there is no doubt ‘they prayed by a special movement of the Holy Spirit,’ for God does not permit all simply to pray for bodily healing; a rule is prescribed ‘what we ought to ask,’ from which we depart only when the Spirit suggests a special prayer. Christ puts the question ‘for the sake of all the people,’ to awaken the crowd to observe the miracle.

The shared, dominant note is that Christ, though hastening to Jerusalem and his passion, stands still for two blind beggars, and draws out their request not to inform himself but to qualify them — and the watching crowd — for the mercy he is about to give. As Henry observed on the foregoing verses, in prayer ‘we do not draw the mercy to ourselves, but ourselves to the mercy.’ The witnesses agree that Christ, though hastening to Jerusalem and his passion, stands still for two blind beggars — a proof of the regard he has to faith and prayer. All concur that he draws out their request not to inform himself but to qualify them, and the watching crowd, for the mercy he is about to give; as Calvin notes, they prayed by a special movement of the Spirit, and the question awakens the bystanders to mark the miracle that follows.
33:
‘They say unto him, Lord, that our eyes may be opened.’ The few witnesses, grammatical-historical with the Reformed Gill, mark the definiteness of the request and the faith behind it.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the plain petition. Barnes and Clarke observe that the blind men ask not for alms but for sight: ‘a hungry man can easily ask bread… the afflicted heart alone can tell its own sorrows.’ Ellicott records that Mark preserves the Hebrew word actually uttered, Rabboni (compare John 20:16), a title of deep reverence.

The Reformed Gill draws out the faith: that they asked for the recovery of sight, not alms, shows they ‘made no doubt’ of Christ’s power, and that the opening of the eyes of the blind was prophesied as a work of the Messiah (Isaiah 35:5) — a prophecy that may have ‘encouraged their faith to expect a cure from him.’ They sought sight so that they might ‘get their bread in another way,’ not choosing, like idle beggars, to remain under their calamity.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the blind men know exactly what they want and ask it directly of one they believe able to grant it. Their request — ‘that our eyes may be opened’ — is the answer to Christ’s question of v.32, and the whole exchange becomes, in the hands of these expositors, a model of believing prayer: definite in its asking, reverent in its address (‘Lord,’ Rabboni), and grounded in the conviction that the Son of David is both able and willing to give sight to the blind, as the prophets had foretold of the Messiah’s day. The witnesses agree the blind men know exactly what they want and ask it directly of one they believe able to grant it; their petition is the answer to Christ’s question, definite in its asking, reverent in its address, and grounded in the conviction that the Son of David can open blind eyes, as the prophets had foretold of the Messiah’s day.
34:
‘So Jesus had compassion on them, and touched their eyes: and immediately they received sight, and they followed him.’ The witnesses agree on the compassion, the miracle, and the following that crowned it.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress pity and proof. Clarke and Robertson note the verb of tender compassion (the ‘bowels’ of mercy) and that the touch ‘would soothe the eyes as they were healed.’ Barnes adds from Mark and Luke the word ‘Thy faith hath saved thee’: faith ‘had no power to open the eyes, but it led them to Jesus,’ and he marshals the marks of an undoubted miracle — the men well known, strangers to Jesus, healed before deeply interested multitudes who ‘could easily have detected the imposition.’ Ellicott observes that compassion, not the popular demand for signs, drew out the work.

The Reformed Calvin reads the following as ‘an expression of gratitude’: the healed men became disciples, presenting themselves ‘as mirrors of the grace of Christ’ and so confirming the certainty of the miracle. Gill marks the compassion shown ‘by some outward sign.’

The Wesleyan Clarke draws the appeal: ‘whosoever thou art, act in behalf of thy soul as these blind men did in behalf of their sight… he is passing by, and thou art passing into eternity.’ Barnes’s extended remarks on the chapter gather its lessons — that reward goes by the manner, not merely the time, of service; that men are by nature spiritually blind and must cry to Christ for sight; that present opportunity must be seized, since this was Christ’s last passage through Jericho; and that the healed should follow him. The shared, dominant note is that Christ’s tender compassion meets persevering faith with a complete cure, and that the proper response to sight restored is to follow the Giver — for ‘none follow Christ blindly’ (compare Luke 18:43). The witnesses agree that Christ’s tender compassion meets persevering faith with a complete and undoubted cure, and that the fitting response to sight restored is to follow the Giver; faith, all concur, had no power to open the eyes but led the men to him who had, so that the whole miracle becomes a parable of the spiritually blind who cry to Christ and, receiving sight, follow him.
